Walk through each argument carefully. This is the code students will write every week from now on.

formula: the tilde syntax encodes the linear predictor. `y ~ x` means "model y as a function of x." brms assumes a Gaussian likelihood by default (this will change in week 10 when we use binary outcomes). The intercept is included automatically.

prior: this is where we specify P(θ). The three priors here are weakly informative:
- Normal(0, 5) for the intercept: allows values from roughly -10 to +10 — broad but not flat
- Normal(0, 2) for the slope: allows effects from roughly -4 to +4 — reasonable for standardized predictors
- Exponential(1) for sigma: positive, with prior mass concentrated below 5 but allowing larger values

chains, iter, warmup: the computational setup. Four chains, 2000 iterations each, first 1000 discarded. Total posterior draws = 4 × 1000 = 4000.

seed: critical for reproducibility. When we say "the analysis was run with seed 2026," anyone can reproduce the exact same chain paths and therefore the exact same posterior samples. This connects back to the reproducible research theme from week 2.

brms → Stan → C++: the compilation takes a few seconds (or up to a minute for complex models). After compilation, sampling is fast. The compilation happens once; subsequent fits with the same model structure use a cached version.
:::

---

## Prior Specification {.smaller}

Always verify the priors brms is using:

```{r}
#| label: prior-summary
#| echo: true
#| eval: true

get_prior(formula = y ~ x, data = df_synth)
```

. . .

```{r}
#| label: prior-summary-fit
#| echo: true
#| eval: true

prior_summary(fit_synth)
```

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.72em"}
`get_prior()` shows what priors are available to set. `prior_summary()` shows what was actually used. Always check both: brms fills in defaults for any unspecified parameter. Understanding your priors is part of understanding your model.
:::

::: notes
Prior transparency is a key Bayesian principle: every analysis makes prior assumptions. Bayesian analysis makes them explicit and auditable. These two commands make the priors visible before and after fitting.

get_prior(): shows all parameters in the model and which prior classes they belong to. The "class" column distinguishes: Intercept (the α parameter), b (regression slopes like β), and sigma (the residual standard deviation).

prior_summary(): shows the actual priors used in the fitted model. If the student specified priors, these appear here. If brms filled in defaults (e.g., a flat prior or a weakly informative prior), those appear too.

The pedagogical point: you should always be able to answer "what did I assume before seeing the data?" for every parameter in your model. Prior specification is a scientific decision that should be justified in your write-up.

Note for students: if you fit a model and forget to check, you might be using flat priors (which are often inadvisable) or brms defaults that don't match your scientific understanding. check_prior() = check your assumptions.
:::

---

## The `summary()` Output {.smaller}

```{r}
#| label: brms-summary
#| echo: true
#| eval: true

summary(fit_synth)
```

::: notes
Walk through the output line by line. This is the most important thing to be able to read and interpret.

The header: model formula, number of observations, number of families (likelihood families), links (identity = Gaussian with no transformation), chains (4), iterations (2000), warmup (1000).

The Population-Level Effects table (the fixed effects): one row per parameter.
- Estimate: posterior mean (the "best single guess")
- Est.Error: posterior standard deviation (uncertainty)
- l-95% CI and u-95% CI: 95% credible interval — there is 95% posterior probability that the true parameter lies in this range, given data and prior
- Rhat: should be 1.00 for all parameters
- Bulk_ESS and Tail_ESS: should be well above 400

Check recovery: Intercept ≈ 2? b_x ≈ 0.7? sigma ≈ 1? The posterior means should be close to the true values, with credible intervals that contain the true values.

Family Specific Parameters: sigma (the residual standard deviation) — this should be ≈ 1.

This is a beautiful example of Bayesian inference working: the posterior means approximately recover the true data-generating parameters, with uncertainty captured by the credible intervals. The Rhat of 1.00 and high ESS indicate the chains converged well.
:::

---

## Trace Plots: Diagnosing Convergence {.smaller}

```{r}
#| label: fig-brms-trace
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| fig-height: 3.5
#| fig-cap: "Trace plots for the three model parameters. Four chains in different colours. All chains are stationary and overlapping — the hairy caterpillar."

mcmc_plot(
  fit_synth,
  type = "trace",
  variable = c("b_Intercept", "b_x", "sigma")
)
```

::: notes
Walk through the trace plots. There are three panels, one per parameter.

Each panel: x-axis = iteration number (1 to 1000, post-warm-up only), y-axis = parameter value. Four colored lines correspond to the four chains.

What to look for:
1. Stationarity: the mean and spread of each chain should not trend over time. Each chain looks like white noise around a stable level.
2. Mixing: the four chains should be interleaved — you should not be able to visually identify which chain is which by color (they should all overlap). 
3. Consistent levels: all four chains should be exploring the same region of parameter space.

This looks like the "hairy caterpillar" from the conceptual section. The chains for b_Intercept are centered around ≈ 2, b_x around ≈ 0.7, sigma around ≈ 1 — matching the true data-generating values.

Compare to the bad trace plot from Part VI: no separation, no trending, no stickiness. This is what we want.

R-hat values from summary(): all 1.00. This is consistent with what we see visually. Remember: the visual check and the numerical check are complementary — both should be performed.
:::

---

## Posterior Distributions {.smaller}

```{r}
#| label: fig-brms-posterior
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| fig-height: 3.2
#| fig-cap: "Posterior distributions for the three model parameters. Shaded region = 95% credible interval. Vertical line = posterior median. True values (α=2, β=0.7, σ=1) lie well within the posteriors."

mcmc_plot(
  fit_synth,
  type = "areas",
  variable = c("b_Intercept", "b_x", "sigma"),
  prob = 0.95
) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = c(2, 0.7, 1),
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = col_2,
    linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  labs(subtitle = "Dashed lines = true data-generating parameter values")
```

::: notes
The posterior area plots are the core deliverable of Bayesian inference. Each panel shows the full posterior distribution for one parameter.

What each element means:
- The curve: the full posterior density p(θ|data). This is what Bayes' theorem produces.
- The shaded region: the 95% credible interval. There is 95% posterior probability that the true parameter value lies in this range.
- The vertical line: the posterior median (approximately equal to the posterior mean for symmetric posteriors).
- The orange dashed lines: true parameter values (added by us, possible only because we generated the data).

Interpretation for each parameter:
- b_Intercept: centered around ≈ 2, with 95% CI containing 2.0. Recovery successful.
- b_x: centered around ≈ 0.7, with 95% CI containing 0.7. Recovery successful.
- sigma: centered around ≈ 1, with 95% CI containing 1.0. Recovery successful.

Key point: the posteriors are full distributions, not point estimates. We could compute any posterior quantity from these samples: the probability that β > 0, the probability that β is between 0.5 and 1.0, the expected value of y for a new x value. All are available from the samples without additional computation.

This is the payoff of the Bayesian workflow: not just a coefficient and a p-value, but a full probability distribution representing our updated beliefs about each parameter.
:::

---

## Posterior Predictive Check {.smaller}

```{r}
#| label: fig-pp-check
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| fig-height: 3.0
#| fig-cap: "Posterior predictive check: 50 datasets simulated from posterior draws (light blue) versus the observed data (dark blue). The observed data distribution is well within the range of simulated datasets — the model fits."

pp_check(fit_synth, ndraws = 50)
```

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.78em"}
**Logic:** draw parameter values $\theta^{(s)}$ from the posterior → simulate a new dataset $y^{\text{rep}}$ from $P(y \mid \theta^{(s)})$ → compare simulated datasets to the observed data $y$.

If the model is well-specified, observed data should look like a *typical draw* from the posterior predictive distribution. A systematic discrepancy indicates model misspecification.
:::

::: notes
The posterior predictive check is a model fit assessment tool. It uses the full generative power of the Bayesian model: once we have posterior samples, we can simulate new datasets by plugging the samples into the data-generating model.

Interpretation: the light blue lines are distributions of 50 simulated datasets. The dark blue line is the observed data. If the model fits well, the dark blue line should be similar to the light blue lines — the observed data should look like a typical draw from the model.

Here: the observed distribution (a roughly bell-shaped distribution of y values) is well within the range of simulated distributions. The model captures the central tendency, spread, and shape of the data. This is a successful posterior predictive check.

What a failed pp_check looks like: a systematic discrepancy between the observed and simulated distributions. For example, if the observed data is bimodal but all simulated distributions are unimodal, the Gaussian likelihood is misspecified.

This is a preview of model checking tools — we will develop more sophisticated checks in weeks 8–13. The key takeaway: the posterior is not just for reading parameters; it is generative and can be used to simulate new data for model validation.
:::

---

## Reading the Output: What Just Happened {.smaller}

Connecting the `brms` output to the MCMC machinery:

. . .

- The **4,000 rows** in the posterior are samples from **4 chains × 1,000 post-warm-up steps**
- Each step used the **NUTS algorithm** — a frictionless ball rolling on the negative log-posterior surface — to propose an accepted move
- **Convergence** was assessed by $\hat{R}$ (≈ 1.00 for all parameters) and visually by trace plots (stationary, mixed chains)
- The **posterior distributions** directly encode our updated beliefs about $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\sigma$ after combining the prior and the likelihood
- $P(\text{data})$ **was never computed**. It cancelled in every acceptance ratio

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.72em"}
When `brms` prints `Compiling Stan program...` and `Chain 1: Iteration 1/2000`, you now know exactly what is happening and why. Next week, the model structure becomes more interesting. The MCMC machinery runs identically underneath.
:::

::: notes
This closing slide for the brms section connects the abstract machinery to the concrete output. Walk through each bullet and explicitly trace it back to a section of the session:

"4,000 rows" → Part VI: multiple chains, warm-up, standard setup
"Each step used NUTS" → Part V: the ball on the landscape, HMC
"Convergence assessed by R-hat" → Part VI: diagnostics, falsificationism
"Posterior distributions" → Part IV/V: the stationary distribution of the Markov Chain, the Monte Carlo estimator
"P(data) was never computed" → Part IV: the key algebraic insight from the Island King

The callout box closes the loop: students who have seen this session will understand what brms is doing when it runs. They are no longer users of a black box — they are users of a machine whose internal logic they understand.

This understanding matters for:
1. Diagnosing problems (divergences, low ESS, high R-hat)
2. Making sensible modeling decisions (prior choice, model structure)
3. Interpreting output correctly (posterior means and intervals as probability statements)
4. Communicating results to nontechnical audiences
:::

# Bridge to Week 8

## From Infrastructure to Science {.smaller}

Today was about the **plumbing**. From next week onward, the plumbing runs invisibly.

. . .

The MCMC machinery is **model-agnostic**: the same sampling algorithm runs regardless of the model. What changes is the log-posterior function — determined by your prior and likelihood choices.

. . .

The `brms` workflow is now fixed:

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
1. Specify the likelihood family and model structure via `formula`
2. Specify priors via `prior` — and justify them
3. Run 4 chains, check $\hat{R}$, ESS, and trace plots
4. Interpret the posterior distributions as probability statements about parameters
5. Use posterior predictive checks to assess model fit
:::

. . .

**Coming weeks:**

- **Week 8:** Bivariate linear regression — one predictor, one outcome, causal and descriptive interpretation
- **Week 9:** Multivariate regression and interactions — controlling for confounders, moderation effects
- **Week 10:** Binary outcomes — **Bernoulli** likelihood replaces Gaussian; everything else stays identical

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.72em"}
The prior–likelihood–posterior logic does not change. The formula inside the likelihood family does.
:::

::: notes
This slide explicitly names what was learned today (infrastructure) and what comes next (science). The transition is important: students who understood today have the conceptual tools to engage with every model in the course.

The five-step workflow should be committed to memory — it is the standard operating procedure for every empirical analysis in the remainder of the course.

The forward preview: week 10 is the key conceptual test of the Bayesian framework's coherence. The Bernoulli likelihood for binary outcomes has a completely different functional form from the Gaussian, but the prior–posterior update logic is identical. Students who understand MCMC and the three-ingredient structure will find the extension natural. Students who are treating brms as a black box will find it confusing.

Final thought to share: "You now have the tools to understand a Bayesian research paper from a methods perspective. The language of priors, likelihoods, posteriors, R-hat, ESS, and posterior predictive checks is standard in quantitative political science. After today, that language belongs to you."
:::

---

# Thank you for your attention and see you next week!

<a href="https://github.com/dertristan" target="_blank" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 10px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-github" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M8 0C3.58 0 0 3.58 0 8c0 3.54 2.29 6.53 5.47 7.59.4.07.55-.17.55-.38 0-.19-.01-.82-.01-1.49-2.01.37-2.53-.49-2.69-.94-.09-.23-.48-.94-.82-1.13-.28-.15-.68-.52-.01-.53.63-.01 1.08.58 1.23.82.72 1.21 1.87.87 2.33.66.07-.52.28-.87.51-1.07-1.78-.2-3.64-.89-3.64-3.95 0-.87.31-1.59.82-2.15-.08-.2-.36-1.02.08-2.12 0 0 .67-.21 2.2.82.64-.18 1.32-.27 2-.27s1.36.09 2 .27c1.53-1.04 2.2-.82 2.2-.82.44 1.1.16 1.92.08 2.12.51.56.82 1.27.82 2.15 0 3.07-1.87 3.75-3.65 3.95.29.25.54.73.54 1.48 0 1.07-.01 1.93-.01 2.2 0 .21.15.46.55.38A8.01 8.01 0 0 0 16 8c0-4.42-3.58-8-8-8"/> </svg> </a> <a href="mailto:tristan.muno@uni-mannheim.de" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 5px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-envelope" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M0 4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h12a2 2 0 0 1 2 2v8a2 2 0 0 1-2 2H2a2 2 0 0 1-2-2zm2-1a1 1 0 0 0-1 1v.217l7 4.2 7-4.2V4a1 1 0 0 0-1-1zm13 2.383-4.708 2.825L15 11.105zm-.034 6.876-5.64-3.471L8 9.583l-1.326-.795-5.64 3.47A1 1 0 0 0 2 13h12a1 1 0 0 0 .966-.741M1 11.105l4.708-2.897L1 5.383z"/> </svg> </a>

::: {#refs}
:::

# Appendix

## Appendix A: Metropolis Algorithm — Full Pseudocode {.smaller}

The complete Metropolis algorithm for Bayesian inference:

::: {style="font-size: 0.7em;"}

**Input:** unnormalized posterior $\tilde{p}(\theta) = P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)$, proposal distribution $q(\theta^* \mid \theta^{(t)})$, starting point $\theta^{(1)}$, number of iterations $T$.

**For** $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$:

1. **Propose:** draw $\theta^*$ from the proposal distribution $q(\theta^* \mid \theta^{(t)})$

2. **Compute acceptance ratio:**
$$
r = \min\!\left(1,\; \frac{\tilde{p}(\theta^*)}{\tilde{p}(\theta^{(t)})}\right) = \min\!\left(1,\; \frac{P(\text{data} \mid \theta^*) \cdot P(\theta^*)}{P(\text{data} \mid \theta^{(t)}) \cdot P(\theta^{(t)})}\right)
$$

3. **Accept or reject:** draw $u \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)$
   - If $u \leq r$: set $\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^*$ (accept)
   - If $u > r$: set $\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)}$ (reject, stay)

**Output:** sequence $\theta^{(1)}, \theta^{(2)}, \ldots, \theta^{(T)}$ — samples from the stationary distribution $P(\theta \mid \text{data})$ (after warm-up).

:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.68em"}
**Why does this work?** The acceptance rule satisfies **detailed balance**: for any two states $\theta$ and $\theta'$, the probability of being in state $\theta$ and moving to $\theta'$ equals the probability of being in $\theta'$ and moving back to $\theta$:
$$
\pi(\theta) \cdot P(\theta \to \theta') = \pi(\theta') \cdot P(\theta' \to \theta)
$$
Detailed balance implies that $\pi(\theta) = P(\theta \mid \text{data})$ is the stationary distribution. This is the mathematical guarantee that the Metropolis algorithm produces posterior samples.
:::

::: notes
This appendix slide is for students who want the formal underpinning. Present it briefly if time permits, or point students to it as a reference.

The key formal result: detailed balance is a sufficient condition for a Markov Chain to have a given stationary distribution. The Metropolis acceptance rule is constructed precisely to satisfy detailed balance for the target posterior.

Verification of detailed balance for the Metropolis rule: consider two states θ and θ'. Without loss of generality, assume p(θ') > p(θ) (so θ' is more probable).

P(θ → θ') = q(θ'|θ) × min(1, p(θ')/p(θ)) = q(θ'|θ) × 1 = q(θ'|θ) [since p(θ') > p(θ)]
P(θ' → θ) = q(θ|θ') × min(1, p(θ)/p(θ')) = q(θ|θ') × p(θ)/p(θ') [since p(θ) < p(θ')]

For symmetric proposal (q(θ'|θ) = q(θ|θ')):
π(θ) × P(θ → θ') = p(θ) × q(θ'|θ) [since π = p]
π(θ') × P(θ' → θ) = p(θ') × q(θ|θ') × p(θ)/p(θ') = p(θ) × q(θ|θ') = p(θ) × q(θ'|θ) ✓

So detailed balance holds, and the stationary distribution is p(θ|data). QED.

HMC satisfies a more general version of detailed balance via the volume-preserving and reversible Hamiltonian dynamics. The proof is more involved and uses symplectic geometry.
:::

---

## Appendix B: MCMC Algorithm Comparison {.smaller}

::: {style="font-size: 0.65em;"}

| Algorithm | Year | Proposal | Key innovation | Limitation | Used in |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Metropolis** | 1953 | Symmetric random walk | First MCMC algorithm; simple acceptance rule | Slow mixing; requires tuning of step size | Historical; rarely used in practice today |
| **Metropolis-Hastings** | 1970 | Asymmetric proposals allowed | Generalises Metropolis to arbitrary proposals | Still requires manual tuning | Textbooks; simple models |
| **Gibbs Sampler** | 1984/1990 | Conditional distributions | Acceptance rate = 1; dimension-by-dimension | Requires tractable conditionals; slow on correlated posteriors | BUGS, JAGS |
| **HMC** | 1987/2011 | Leapfrog trajectory (gradient-guided) | Uses gradient information; large, efficient jumps | Requires differentiable posterior; trajectory length must be tuned | Stan (basic HMC) |
| **NUTS** | 2014 | Adaptive leapfrog (no U-turn) | Eliminates trajectory length tuning; full automation | Computationally expensive per step vs. random walk | **Stan (default) → `brms`** |

:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.68em"}
`brms` uses **NUTS** via Stan for all models. The progression from Metropolis to NUTS represents 60 years of algorithmic development: each generation solved the main limitation of the previous one. NUTS is currently the state of the art for general-purpose Bayesian computation with continuous parameters.

References: @metropolis1953equation; @gelfand1990sampling; @hoffman2014no; @burkner2017brms.
:::

::: notes
This reference table is for students who want to situate today's content in the broader MCMC literature.

Metropolis (1953): the original. Symmetric random-walk proposal. The island king story with equal-probability left/right proposals.

Metropolis-Hastings (1970, Hastings): generalized to asymmetric proposals q(θ'|θ) ≠ q(θ|θ'). The acceptance ratio includes a correction factor for the proposal asymmetry. Nearly all MCMC algorithms today are Metropolis-Hastings variants.

Gibbs Sampler (Geman & Geman 1984 for images; Gelfand & Smith 1990 for statistics): update each parameter one at a time by sampling from its full conditional distribution p(θ_j | θ_{-j}, data). Always accepts (no rejection). Efficient when conditionals are tractable; slow for correlated parameters (the chain must make many small moves to traverse the joint posterior).

HMC (Duane et al. 1987 for physics; Neal 2011 for statistics): introduced gradient information. The leapfrog integrator simulates Hamiltonian dynamics. Requires manual tuning of step size ε and number of leapfrog steps L.

NUTS (Hoffman & Gelman 2014): automated HMC. Uses the "no U-turn" criterion to adaptively choose trajectory length. During warm-up, also adapts step size and mass matrix. Full automation makes it suitable for general-purpose use without domain expertise in MCMC tuning. This is what Stan implements.

For students interested in the history: the BUGS and JAGS software packages (popular before Stan) used Gibbs sampling and were the dominant tools for Bayesian computation from about 1990 to 2012. Stan's NUTS represented a generational change in computational efficiency.
:::
